items.prompt_md: Before merging a list of unsorted, possibly-overlapping intervals, what preprocessing step makes a single linear sweep sufficient?
options: Sort the intervals by their start value
Sort the intervals by their length
Reverse the list so the last interval is processed first
Deduplicate any intervals with identical start values
items.answer: Sort the intervals by their start value
items.explanation_md: Once sorted by start, any interval that could still overlap the one you're building must appear immediately next, so one pass suffices.

items.prompt_md: While sweeping start-sorted intervals and holding a running "current merged interval," what test decides whether the next interval should be absorbed into it?
options: The next interval's start is at or before the current merged interval's end
The next interval's start is strictly greater than the current merged interval's start
The next interval's end is at or before the current merged interval's start
The next interval is shorter than the current merged interval
items.answer: The next interval's start is at or before the current merged interval's end
items.explanation_md: That's the overlap condition once intervals are start-sorted: start <= running end means the two ranges touch or cross, so they belong in one merged interval.

items.prompt_md: When two intervals touch exactly at a boundary, e.g. [1, 5] and [5, 8], should they be merged?
options: Yes — sharing an endpoint still counts as overlapping for a merge, so they should combine into [1, 8]
No — they must stay separate because their ranges don't share any interior point
Only if the second interval's start is strictly less than the first interval's end
It depends on whether the intervals are given as floats or integers
items.answer: Yes — sharing an endpoint still counts as overlapping for a merge, so they should combine into [1, 8]
items.explanation_md: The merge condition is start <= prev_end (not strictly less than), so touching endpoints are treated as one continuous covered range.

items.prompt_md: When the current interval does NOT overlap the running merged interval, what should the sweep do?
options: Emit the running merged interval as finished, then start a new running interval from the current one
Discard the current interval since it can't belong to the answer
Extend the running merged interval's end anyway, to be safe
Restart the sweep from the beginning of the list
items.answer: Emit the running merged interval as finished, then start a new running interval from the current one
items.explanation_md: A gap means the running interval is fully closed off — nothing later can reach back and merge with it — so it's safe to output and move on.

items.prompt_md: When stretching the running merged interval's end to absorb an overlapping interval, why must you take the max of the two ends rather than always using the new interval's end?
options: A later-starting interval can still have an earlier end than the one already absorbed, e.g. [1, 10] followed by [2, 3]
Taking the max is only a performance optimization, not a correctness requirement
The new interval's end is always larger, so max is equivalent to just using it
Because intervals must be merged in descending order of end value
items.answer: A later-starting interval can still have an earlier end than the one already absorbed, e.g. [1, 10] followed by [2, 3]
items.explanation_md: Sorting only fixes start order, not end order — a nested interval like [2, 3] would shrink the merged range if you didn't keep the max end.

items.prompt_md: Insert Interval gives you a list that is already sorted and non-overlapping, plus one new interval to add. Why can you skip the sort step entirely here?
options: The list's sortedness is a precondition of the problem, so re-sorting would be wasted, correctness-preserving work
Insert Interval never requires merging, only splicing at a fixed position
Because there is only one new interval, sorting has no effect on the result
You still must sort, just by end instead of by start
items.answer: The list's sortedness is a precondition of the problem, so re-sorting would be wasted, correctness-preserving work
items.explanation_md: The merge lesson sorts because the input arrives unordered; Insert Interval's input is guaranteed pre-sorted, so a single linear walk is enough.

items.prompt_md: In Insert Interval, which intervals from the existing sorted list get copied to the output completely untouched?
options: Every interval that ends strictly before the new interval's start
Every interval that starts before the new interval's start
Every interval, since the new interval always needs the whole list re-merged
Only the very first interval in the list
items.answer: Every interval that ends strictly before the new interval's start
items.explanation_md: An interval that finishes before the new one even begins can't overlap it, so it's safe to output as-is before any absorbing begins.

items.prompt_md: What is the overall time complexity of merging n unsorted, possibly-overlapping intervals?
options: O(n log n), dominated by the initial sort, since the sweep itself is O(n)
O(n), because the sweep alone determines the running time
O(n²), because every interval must be compared against every other
O(log n), since intervals can be located with binary search
items.answer: O(n log n), dominated by the initial sort, since the sweep itself is O(n)
items.explanation_md: The linear sweep is cheap; the sort by start is the expensive step, so O(n log n) sets the overall bound.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of inserting one new interval into an already sorted, non-overlapping list of n intervals?
options: O(n), a single linear walk with no sorting needed
O(n log n), since the list must be re-sorted after insertion
O(log n), via binary search alone
O(n²), since the new interval may overlap every existing one
items.answer: O(n), a single linear walk with no sorting needed
items.explanation_md: Because the list is already sorted and non-overlapping, one pass — copy, absorb, copy — handles the insertion without any sort.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the brute-force alternative that checks every pair of intervals for overlap before merging?
options: O(n²), since each interval is compared against every other interval
O(n log n), matching the sort-based approach
O(n), since overlap can be checked in constant time overall
O(2^n), since every subset of intervals must be tried
items.answer: O(n²), since each interval is compared against every other interval
items.explanation_md: Without a sort to localize which intervals can possibly overlap, an all-pairs check costs quadratic time — exactly what sorting avoids.

items.prompt_md: What is the auxiliary space complexity of the standard merge-intervals sweep, ignoring the output list itself?
options: O(1) extra beyond the output, since only a running start/end pair is tracked
O(n), because a hash map of every interval is required
O(n log n), matching the sort's space usage in the worst case
O(n²), because all pairs must be stored for comparison
items.answer: O(1) extra beyond the output, since only a running start/end pair is tracked
items.explanation_md: Besides the output list (and whatever the sort itself allocates internally), the sweep only needs a couple of variables for the current merged interval.

items.prompt_md: A buggy merge implementation sorts intervals by their **end** value instead of their **start** value before sweeping. What goes wrong?
options: The sweep can process an interval before one that overlaps it but starts earlier, wrongly leaving them unmerged
Nothing goes wrong — sorting by end also groups overlapping intervals correctly
It merges every interval into a single range regardless of gaps
It only fails when the intervals are already sorted by start
items.answer: The sweep can process an interval before one that overlaps it but starts earlier, wrongly leaving them unmerged
items.explanation_md: The linear sweep's correctness depends on encountering intervals in start order; sorting by end breaks the guarantee that overlap can only be with the immediately preceding running interval.

items.prompt_md: You're given a list of intervals, some overlapping, and must return the minimal set of intervals that covers the same ranges.
options: Sort intervals by start, then merge/sweep
Min-heap of end times
Brute-force all pairs
Hash map
items.answer: Sort intervals by start, then merge/sweep
items.explanation_md: "Collapse overlapping ranges into the smallest equivalent set" is the sort-by-start-and-sweep trigger — no live count of concurrent ranges is needed, just a running merged interval.

items.prompt_md: You have an already sorted, non-overlapping list of intervals and must splice in one new interval, fixing up whatever it now touches.
options: Sort intervals by start, then merge/sweep
Walk the sorted list once: copy untouched, absorb overlaps, copy the rest
Brute-force all pairs
Hash map
items.answer: Walk the sorted list once: copy untouched, absorb overlaps, copy the rest
items.explanation_md: The list is already sorted, so re-sorting is wasted work — a single linear walk applying the same merge rule handles the one insertion.

items.prompt_md: Given a stack of calendar events that may overlap, you need the fewest number of blocks that still cover every event's time span.
options: Sort intervals by start, then merge/sweep
Min-heap of end times
Brute-force all pairs
Two-pointer scan from both ends
items.answer: Sort intervals by start, then merge/sweep
items.explanation_md: "Fewest blocks that cover every span" is the merge-intervals goal — sort by start and fuse whatever touches or crosses, in one pass.

items.prompt_md: At every moment during the day, you need to know exactly how many meetings are simultaneously in progress, updated as each meeting starts and ends.
options: Sort intervals by start, then merge/sweep
Min-heap of end times
Brute-force all pairs
Hash map
items.answer: Min-heap of end times
items.explanation_md: Trap: this asks for a live, constantly-updated concurrent count, not a static merged range — that calls for a min-heap of end times (or an end-vs-start sweep), not a single merged-interval pass.

items.prompt_md: You're given thousands of overlapping shift schedules and need to merge them into the smallest set of non-overlapping shift blocks, as fast as possible.
options: Sort intervals by start, then merge/sweep
Brute-force all pairs
Hash map
Recursion over every subset of shifts
items.answer: Sort intervals by start, then merge/sweep
items.explanation_md: Merging overlapping ranges into a minimal covering set is always sort-by-start-then-sweep — an O(n log n) approach, unlike the exponential or quadratic alternatives listed.

items.prompt_md: You must assign a room number to each meeting request such that no two meetings sharing a room overlap, and you want the minimum number of rooms actually needed at any instant.
options: Sort intervals by start, then merge/sweep
Min-heap of end times
Brute-force all pairs
Hash map
items.answer: Min-heap of end times
items.explanation_md: Trap: minimum simultaneous rooms needed tracks how many intervals are live at once — pop the heap's earliest end whenever a new meeting starts before it, which is a heap problem, not a merge.

items.prompt_md: Given an unsorted array of number ranges representing sensor active-windows, produce the smallest list of windows that covers the exact same total time with no gaps merged incorrectly.
options: Sort intervals by start, then merge/sweep
Brute-force all pairs
Hash map
Sort by value and binary search each range
items.answer: Sort intervals by start, then merge/sweep
items.explanation_md: "Smallest list of windows covering the same total time" is the merge-intervals task, solved by sorting on start and sweeping once.

items.prompt_md: You're given two separate lists of intervals — your free time-slots and a colleague's free time-slots — each already sorted internally by start, and must find every overlapping slot between the two lists.
options: Walk both lists at once with two pointers, always advancing whichever current interval ends first
Sort intervals by start, then merge/sweep
Brute-force all pairs
Min-heap of end times
items.answer: Walk both lists at once with two pointers, always advancing whichever current interval ends first
items.explanation_md: Trap: with two already-sorted lists to reconcile against each other, a two-pointer merge (advance the interval with the smaller end) finds every cross-list overlap in one pass — no re-sorting or single-list sweep needed.

items.prompt_md: You receive a large batch of unsorted appointment ranges from many sources and must produce the smallest possible set of ranges that together cover exactly the same total time.
options: Sort intervals by start, then merge/sweep
Min-heap of end times
Brute-force all pairs
Two pointers converging from both ends
items.answer: Sort intervals by start, then merge/sweep
items.explanation_md: "Smallest set of ranges covering the same total time" from an unsorted batch is the merge-intervals goal — sort by start, fuse whatever touches or overlaps, in one O(n log n) pass.

items.prompt_md: You maintain a sorted, non-overlapping list of booked time slots and must repeatedly insert new individual bookings one at a time, fixing up overlaps after each insertion.
options: Walk the sorted list once per insertion: copy untouched, absorb overlaps, copy the rest
Sort the entire list from scratch after every insertion
Brute-force all pairs after every insertion
Min-heap of end times
items.answer: Walk the sorted list once per insertion: copy untouched, absorb overlaps, copy the rest
items.explanation_md: Since the list stays sorted and non-overlapping between insertions, each new booking only needs the same O(n) single-pass splice as the Insert Interval problem — re-sorting from scratch would waste the invariant already held.
